This check is not appropriate. Simply declare what types are acceptable in the docstring, then assume that the values are OK. By doing this check, you are preventing the user from using other perfectly legal data types, such as `namedtuple` or custom classes that define logic necessary for indexing.

Remember that Python is a duck-typed language. It is rarely a good idea to check for an explicit set of classes. If you must make any sort of check at all, then check for *behaviour*, such as the ability to index.

@eric-haibin-lin Yes, we need to construct the table every time. Our current implementation is to first construct a cumsum table and then search the columns to get the result. The search complexity of the current implementation is O(N) and the best possible search complexity if O(log(N)).  For this approach, the construction complexity is O(N) and the search complexity is O(1), which will faster when we draw multiple samples from the same multinomial distribution.

This is a spot where angular can make your life LOTS easier.  We actually don't need this function at all.  Here's where you want to assign a value to the scope that keeps track of which suggested product is currently selected.  You can set it and get it just by looking at ``$scope.selectedProduct``.  I'll put comments in the spots where the changes are needed, but here's a summary:

After you get the results of the product search in ``findProduct``, you loop through the results and build your ``suggestedProducts``.  After that loop, add a line:

     $scope.selectedProduct = $scope.suggestedProducts[0];

Then, in the HTML for the ``ng-repeat`` for these options, take out the ``ng-init`` and for each ``<input>`` element, add in ``ng-model="selectedProduct"``

then down in ``submitFilter`` you can just get the value of ``$scope.selectedProduct`` and split it or give an error if it's not set or what have you.

I chimed in on this in the meeting, but perhaps starting to adopt the `npm` opinion now would be in order. Let's please not keep making new canonical locations and structures for the same string. 

Adopting this approach, once little extensions grow up and leave the nest, they already have all of their metadata ready to go, and could in turn be used by other kernels.

Instead of `_jupyter_nbextension_paths`, perhaps `_jupyter_nbextension_packages`, where each of the paths returned must contain a `package.json`. In each of these, go ahead and claim the `jupyter` namespace, and have the dictionary that is currently returned by the python magic name instead be stored here.

Here is an extreme way to encode all that information:

```python
# __init__.py
def _jupyter_nbextension_paths():
    # yeah, cuz windows
    return [
        ["static", "some-extension"]
    ]
```

```yaml
# package.json
{
  "name": "some-extension", # this is the install destination name
  "version": "0.1.0",
  "jupyter": {
    "4": { # just be ready
      "nbextension": {
        "section": "notebook",
        "src": ["."], # path bits, relative to this file
        "require": "dist/index",
      }
    }
  }
  # ... all the other package stuff
}
```

Granted, this would be a bit weird for those of us that: 
- have "mixed" repos that have `package.json` and `setup.py` at the same level
  - _stripping out `package.json` is a pretty gnarly anti-pattern as we are starting to think about versioning_
- use one `package.json` to configure multiple extensions
  - _this is not the node way..._
  - _but there's nothing stopping you from keeping this pattern, and just having/building extra `package.json`s_

 Starting to reduce the amount of redundancy and freedom of choice (to fail) in configuration would probably be worth it to me.

It's a slight performance gain. In a tight loop, `list.pop()` vs `list_pop`, the latter will be faster. 

```python
def foo():
    l = []
    while True:
        l.append(1)
```

The disassembly produces:

```
  2           0 BUILD_LIST               0
              3 STORE_FAST               0 (l)

  3           6 SETUP_LOOP              23 (to 32)
        >>    9 LOAD_GLOBAL              0 (True)
             12 POP_JUMP_IF_FALSE       31

  4          15 LOAD_FAST                0 (l)
             18 LOAD_ATTR                1 (append)
             21 LOAD_CONST               1 (1)
             24 CALL_FUNCTION            1
             27 POP_TOP
             28 JUMP_ABSOLUTE            9
        >>   31 POP_BLOCK
        >>   32 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             35 RETURN_VALUE
```

As for:

```python
def foo():
    l = []
    append = l.append
    while True:
        append(1)
```


```
  2           0 BUILD_LIST               0
              3 STORE_FAST               0 (l)

  3           6 LOAD_FAST                0 (l)
              9 LOAD_ATTR                0 (append)
             12 STORE_FAST               1 (append)

  4          15 SETUP_LOOP              20 (to 38)
        >>   18 LOAD_GLOBAL              1 (True)
             21 POP_JUMP_IF_FALSE       37

  5          24 LOAD_FAST                1 (append)
             27 LOAD_CONST               1 (1)
             30 CALL_FUNCTION            1
             33 POP_TOP
             34 JUMP_ABSOLUTE           18
        >>   37 POP_BLOCK
        >>   38 LOAD_CONST               0 (None)
             41 RETURN_VALUE
```


Notice that in the loop body, `LOAD_FAST` and then `LOAD_ATTR` has to be called. Whereas, `LOAD_FAST` is only called in the second version.

I see.  Unfortunately, I think the spec text is a bit confusing in this case.  The intended behavior is that the device updates the Network Data, Active Dataset, and Pending Dataset all together.  The word `local` in this case is not the same as `mLocal` in the code.  The spec's use of `local` just means the device's copy of the respective objects.

So relative to OpenThread, this means that receiving an MLE Data Response is used primarily to update the `mNetwork` versions of those objects and they must all happen together or none at all.  If any of those timestamps are greater than the `mLocal` versions, than the `mLocal` versions are updated as well.

Does this make sense?

It is not overwritten, but just written into a new logic block. And the parent information which is last written would be retrieved in `otPlatSettingsGet()`. 

The advantage may be not so obvious, but here marking the outdated information with deleted flag in flash would help to improve the efficiency of 
1) getting parent info from flash (false for this `if` to avoid more flash op) https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/utils/settings.cpp#L363)
2) finding the valid information when swapping is required (false for this `if` to avoid extra loop and flash op)
 (https://github.com/openthread/openthread/blob/master/examples/platforms/utils/settings.cpp#L175)
 
It do have some subtle advantages when the device may experience multiple attach processes after long-term run.

```Python
def test_submenu_second_say_from_top_level(submenu_app):
    line = 'testing'
    out1, out2 = run_submenu_cmd(submenu_app, 'second say ' + line)

    # No output expected from the top level
    assert out1 == []

    # Output expected from the second level
    assert len(out2) == 1

```
